Budget per event: USD 35,000–USD 65,000 (scales with number of clients and HJ aggregate value displayed)
Success metric: Conversion rate, average piece value, client satisfaction score

TYPE 2 — WATCH CELEBRATION EVENTS
Format: Private evening (20–35 clients), watch-specific programming. Collaborations with watch journalists, vintage watch collectors, motorsport personalities, or aviation history experts. Hosted in boutique or premium external venue (private member clubs, art galleries, architectural landmarks).
APAC target FY2025: 24 events (minimum 2 per market per year, flagships 4+)
Example events:
- 'Santos at Altitude' — Tokyo: Santos collection evening with aviation heritage curator, vintage cockpit display, co-hosted with Japan Aviators Club
- 'The Art of Complications' — Shanghai: Watch complications demonstration evening with movement displayed under magnification, attended by 28 Tier 1 male clients. Post-event conversion: 4 Santos Skeleton, 1 Rotonde Tourbillon ($165K). ROI: 8.4x event cost.
- 'Women & Watches' — Seoul: Female watch collector evening, challenges the perception that fine watches are a male category. Ballon Bleu and Panthère focused.

TYPE 3 — BRAND EXPERIENCE & HERITAGE EVENTS
Concept: Immersive experiences that deepen emotional connection to the Maison's history and values — not product presentations. No transactional agenda. Build relationship equity.
Examples:
- 'Aurelle & Art' — Partnership with Art Basel Hong Kong: Private collector dinner with curator introduction to artworks that inspired Aurelle design language. 45 Tier 1 clients, no products presented.
- 'Behind the Craft' — Singapore: Live goldsmithing demonstration by Aurelle artisan visiting from France. Clients participate in a workshop component. 20 clients.
- 'Maison Evening' — Annual per-market event: Hosted in a culturally relevant private venue (Tokyo: traditional ryokan salon; Shanghai: restored French Concession villa; Seoul: contemporary architecture gallery). Storytelling, gastronomy, and one signature piece display — not a sales event. The most intimate event in the calendar.

TYPE 4 — GLOBAL EVENTS (INVITATION ONLY)
Watches & Wonders Geneva (April):
APAC VIC invitation quota: 85 clients (Tier 1 Icon only, plus select Tier 1 VIPs). Aurelle covers all travel and accommodation for Tier 1 Icon clients. Tier 1 VIP clients receive preferential access to purchase exhibition tickets independently with priority registration. Geneva experience includes: factory visit, private dinner at Aurelle manufacture, exclusive complication reveal 24 hours before press.

Paris High Jewellery Gala (June, aligned with main collection launch):
APAC quota: 55 clients (primarily Tier 1 Icon and select Tier 1 VIP from HJ-purchasing backgrounds). Held at a significant Parisian landmark (not disclosed until invitation stage). Includes: private viewing of full HJ collection, Michelin-star dinner, gift of collection catalog (museum-quality print edition).

APAC Regional Maison Summit (Q4, rotating city — FY2025: Tokyo):
Annual flagship event gathering Aurelle's top 120 APAC clients for a 2-day luxury cultural program centered on the host city's finest expressions. No product selling — pure brand immersion and appreciation. FY2024 (Seoul): Post-event CLV growth of attending clients averaged +34% in following 12 months.

PARTNERSHIP & CULTURAL PROGRAMMING
Aurelle APAC maintains strategic partnerships for event programming with:
- Four Seasons / Aman / Raffles Hotels: Venue partnership for intimate client events in each key city
- Art Basel HK / Frieze Seoul: Brand presence and private collector activation
- Le Cordon Bleu Tokyo / Bangkok: Culinary partnership for Maison Experience events
- Singapore Tourism Board / Japan National Tourism Agency: For hosting international client groups in the destination
- Formula 1 (Santos/motorsport heritage): APAC race events (Singapore GP, Japanese GP, Australian GP) — corporate hospitality for top clients with Aurelle Santos as thematic connector

EVENT INVITATION PROTOCOL
- All invitations must go through CRM system — no freelance invitations outside the system
- Tier 1 Icon: Personal phone call from Market Director or Boutique Director before written invitation. Written follow-up the same day.
- Tier 1 VIP: Personal phone call or message from Personal Stylist. Written invitation within 24 hours.
- Tier 2: Written invitation via preferred channel (digital or physical). Stylist follow-up message 5 days later.
- RSVP deadline: Minimum 7 days before event. Confirm dietary requirements, preferred language, any accessibility needs.
- Waitlist management: Maintain a waitlist of 30% above capacity — events have a 15% decline rate on average. Do not overcommit beyond 110% of capacity.

title: High Jewellery Launch Protocol — APAC
content:
High Jewellery Collections — APAC Launch & Client Engagement Protocol

OVERVIEW
High Jewellery (HJ) represents Aurelle's highest expression of savoir-faire and the apex of client relationship investment. Each HJ collection launch in APAC is treated as a strategic brand moment — not solely a sales event. The protocol below governs all activities from T-90 days pre-launch through T+30 post-launch, applicable to both annual major collections (Paris Gala equivalent) and seasonal capsule drops.

COLLECTION CALENDAR 2025
- Q1 (March): 'Lumière Éternelle' High Jewellery capsule — 18 pieces, presented at Geneva Watches & Wonders side event for APAC VICs
- Q2 (June): Full annual High Jewellery Collection launch — 64 pieces, Paris Gala followed by APAC touring exhibitions
- Q3 (September): 'Faune Sauvage' animal motif capsule — 22 pieces, targeted at Japanese and Korean markets
- Q4 (November): Private commission season opens — bespoke orders for CNY gifting delivery

PRE-LAUNCH: T-90 TO T-30 DAYS
- T-90: Regional HJ Director and Gemologist team briefed by Paris HQ via secure virtual summit. Product knowledge manuals distributed under NDA — embargo strictly enforced until official reveal date.
- T-90: Personal Stylist briefings commence across all regional flagships. Stylists complete HJ certification module (8 hours, mandatory) before client-facing conversations.
- T-60: VIC invitation list curated jointly by Market Directors and CRM team. Minimum CLV threshold: USD 150,000 for Tier 1 private preview (first-look before any media). USD 80,000 threshold for secondary preview (post media launch, still private).
- T-60: Event logistics finalized — venue dressing, security protocol (all HJ pieces require dual-lock display cases and a dedicated security officer per viewing room), catering, florist, and stylist-to-client ratio confirmed (maximum 3 clients per stylist per session).
- T-30: Personalized invitations dispatched — handwritten notes for CLV >USD 200,000 clients, premium printed invitation for all others. Digital invitations only as backup, never as primary for Tier 1.
- T-30: Media and PR strategy briefed. No social media posts of HJ pieces until official embargo lift. Influencer partnerships (where applicable) governed by separate Media Relations team approval.

PRIVATE VIEWING EVENTS — FORMAT & STANDARDS
Venues: Events held exclusively inside designated flagship Maison Mansion salons:
- Shanghai: Plaza 66 Maison Salon, Level 2 (capacity: 12 clients simultaneously)
- Tokyo: Ginza Mansion Private Floor (capacity: 8 clients, highest per-client service intensity in APAC)
- Seoul: Cheongdam Maison (capacity: 10 clients)
- Hong Kong: Landmark Maison (capacity: 14 clients)
- Singapore: ION Orchard Maison Salon (capacity: 10 clients)
- Beijing: SKP Private Salon (capacity: 8 clients) — operational for major collection launches only
- Sydney: Pitt Street Maison (capacity: 6 clients) — for Q2 and Q4 launches only

Event Format (Standard 90-minute session):
0:00–0:15 — Welcome, champagne or premium non-alcoholic service, ambient introduction by Personal Stylist
0:15–0:45 — Gemologist presentation: collection story, stone sourcing, craftsmanship narrative (15 slides maximum, intimate not corporate)
0:45–1:15 — Individual piece trials, 1-on-1 stylist time, photography upon client consent
1:15–1:30 — Closing, personalized follow-up commitment, gift presentation (collection-branded gift, value approximately USD 150–300)

Staffing Standards:
- 1 Gemologist per event (may be shared across morning/afternoon sessions)
- 1 Personal Stylist per maximum 3 clients (VIP sessions: 1:1 ratio)
- 1 Boutique/Maison Director or Deputy present at all times
- Security: minimum 2 officers for events with pieces valued above USD 500,000 aggregate; 3 officers for aggregate >USD 2M
- Catering: Champagne (Cristal or equivalent), still and sparkling water, seasonal petit fours — sourced locally at Boutique Director's discretion but quality must be pre-approved

PRICING & COMMERCIAL POLICY
- High Jewellery pricing is set strictly at European HQ level, communicated in local currency at prevailing interbank rates updated monthly.
- APAC Market Directors may apply up to a maximum of 3% service adjustment for custom fittings or extensions (e.g., necklace length adjustment, ring resizing above standard).
- Discounts are strictly and absolutely prohibited on any High Jewellery piece — no exceptions regardless of CLV, relationship tenure, or commercial pressure. Any request must be escalated to APAC VP and documented.
- Payment: Bank transfer preferred for transactions above USD 200,000. Credit card acceptance up to USD 100,000 per transaction (market-specific limits apply: Japan USD 50,000, Australia USD 80,000). Escrow arrangements for bespoke commissions.
- Lay-away or reservation: A non-refundable reservation deposit of 20% may be accepted to hold a piece for maximum 45 days. After 45 days, the piece returns to inventory and deposit is converted to a boutique credit.

POST-LAUNCH: T+1 TO T+30
- T+3: Personal thank-you note from Boutique Director to all preview attendees (handwritten).
- T+7: Stylist follow-up call or message to all attendees — assess interest, answer questions, not sales pressure.
- T+14: Any uncommitted pieces from private viewings may be offered to Tier 2 extended preview list.
- T+30: Regional performance debrief — sell-through rate by market, VIC engagement rate, conversion ratio, average transaction value, client feedback synthesis.

title: Human Capital & Talent Strategy APAC
content:
Aurelle APAC Human Capital & Talent Strategy

WORKFORCE OVERVIEW
APAC total headcount: approximately 1,340 (FY2025 plan). Client-facing staff: 1,105 (82.5%). Support functions: 235. The talent strategy recognizes that at Aurelle, every client-facing employee IS the product — the human relationship is as much a part of what clients buy as the physical piece.

TALENT PHILOSOPHY — THE AURELLE APPROACH
We do not hire 'salespeople.' We hire 'relationship architects' who happen to work in a retail environment. The fundamental mindset difference: a salesperson measures success by closing a transaction; an Aurelle stylist measures success by the client returning in 6 months with a different life chapter to share — and a new reason to choose Aurelle for it.

ROLE ARCHITECTURE

Personal Stylist / Client Advisor (CA):
- Entry-level client-facing role. Manages Tier 2 and 3 clients, supports senior stylist on Tier 1 clients.
- Compensation: Base salary + variable (up to 25% of base, tied to: individual revenue, NPS score, CRM compliance rate, repeat client rate). Variable is never called 'commission' internally or externally — 'relationship performance reward.'
- Progression path: CA → Senior CA → VIC Relationship Manager → Client Relations Director

Senior Stylist / VIC Relationship Manager:
- Manages Tier 1 client portfolio (max 60 clients per Senior Stylist, max 25 clients per VIC Manager).
- Has dedicated budget for client relationship gestures (within gifting policy limits).
- Compensation: Base salary + variable (up to 40% of base). At this level, variable is more heavily tied to client lifetime value growth and retention than transactional revenue.
- Expected tenure in role: minimum 3 years (client relationship continuity is non-negotiable — frequent rotation destroys relationship capital).

Watch Specialist:
- Technical specialist role dedicated to watch category. Presents complications, conducts watch-focused client events, supports boutique director during watch manufacturer visits.
- Required: WOSTEP certification or equivalent, or completion of Aurelle Watch Academy (18-month internal program).
- Deployment: 1 per flagship, shared across smaller boutiques on a rotational basis.

Gemologist:
- HJ specialist. Required qualification: GIA GG (Graduate Gemologist) or FGA (Gemmological Association of Great Britain).
- Leads all High Jewellery private viewings. Supports client education on stone quality, origin, treatment disclosure.
- APAC headcount: 18 (5 regional floating Gemologists, 13 boutique-based).

Boutique Director:
- P&L accountable for boutique. Manages all staff, inventory, client experience, and market director relationship.
- Expected profile: minimum 8 years luxury retail, minimum 3 years in management. Strong client network preferred.
- Compensation: Base salary + performance bonus (up to 60% of base tied to: revenue vs. target, repeat client rate, NPS, team retention, mystery shop scores).

TRAINING FRAMEWORK — AURELLE ACADEMY APAC

Foundation Program (Mandatory, all new joiners — 6 weeks):
Week 1–2: Aurelle Maison heritage, founding story, values, creative directors, iconic pieces history
Week 3: Product knowledge — watches fundamentals (movements, collections, key selling stories)
Week 4: Product knowledge — jewellery fundamentals (gemstones, collections, craftsmanship)
Week 5: Client experience standards, CRM systems, communication protocols
Week 6: Market immersion — local consumer culture, competitive landscape, shadowing senior stylist

Specialist Certifications (mandatory within 12 months of joining):
- High Jewellery Certification: 8-hour module, exam required, annual recertification
- Watch Category Certification: 12-hour module, covers movement basics, collection depth, watch care advice
- CRM Mastery: 4-hour module, Salesforce/local platform proficiency, data quality standards
- Client Communication Excellence: Role-play based, 6-hour workshop, conducted in local language

Advanced Programs (for Senior CA and above):
- Aurelle Global Immersion: 2-week program at Paris/Geneva HQ (manufacturing visit, atelier access, global strategy briefing). One cycle per year, 40 participants APAC-wide selected competitively.
- Luxury Leadership Program: Partnered with INSEAD (Singapore campus) — 5-day executive education module for Boutique Directors and above.
- Gemology Sponsorship: Aurelle sponsors GIA GG qualification for identified high-potential Gemologist candidates (1 per market per year).

RECRUITMENT STANDARDS
- Non-negotiable: Genuine passion for the Maison (assessed via 'Aurelle unprompted knowledge' question in first interview)
- Preferred backgrounds: Luxury hospitality (Four Seasons, Aman, Mandarin Oriental) — superior client instincts vs. retail background in many cases. Fine arts or design background adds product narrative depth.
- Language requirements: Minimum bilingual (English + local language). Mandarin strongly preferred for any APAC candidate. Third language (Japanese, Korean, French) provides a significant advantage for VIC role candidates.
- Diversity: Aurelle APAC targets 55% female client-facing staff (reflects primary buyer demographic). All nationalities considered — diversity of cultural fluency is operationally valuable.

RETENTION & RECOGNITION
- Target client-facing turnover: <12% annually (FY2024 actual: 16% — gap driven by Australia 28% and Philippines 22%). Improvement programs underway.
- Career fast-track: Stylists who achieve top NPS and relationship metrics for 2 consecutive years enter the 'Aurelle Talent Accelerator' — a dedicated development and succession planning program.
- Annual Maison Day: One day per year where all APAC staff receive a significant piece from the collection — reinforcing personal connection to the product (equivalent of wearing what you sell).
- Long-service recognition: 5-year, 10-year milestones marked with significant personal gift and a direct letter from APAC President.

title: Market Intelligence — Greater China (Mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau)
content:
Greater China Market Intelligence Report — FY2025 Outlook

MARKET OVERVIEW
Greater China remains Aurelle's largest single market cluster, representing USD 310M of APAC revenue target (50% of regional total). The three sub-markets operate in complementary dynamics: Mainland China drives volume and new client acquisition; Hong Kong serves as a sophisticated cross-border hub; Macau captures tourism-linked luxury spend from Mainland VIPs.

MAINLAND CHINA

Economic Context (FY2025):
Post-COVID recovery has been uneven — high-end luxury (>USD 10,000 transaction value) has proven resilient, while aspirational luxury (USD 1,000–5,000) faces pressure from domestic consumption confidence softness. Aurelle's positioning in the mid-to-high tier is structurally advantageous. GDP growth projected at 4.8%, luxury sector outperforming at +8–10% for established international maisons. Government anti-corruption regulation remains a background consideration — corporate gifting in institutional channels is constrained, but private HNW gifting continues strongly.

Consumer Profile:
- Primary buyer demographic: Female, 28–40, Tier 1–2 cities, professional or entrepreneurial
- Rising segment: Male HNWI 30–45, watches dominant purchase, jewellery gifting
- Second-generation wealth ('Fuerdai'): Cultural consumers, internationally educated, seek brand depth not just logos — Heritage narrative critical
- Geographic wealth shift: Chengdu, Hangzhou, Nanjing, Shenzhen increasingly important — not just Beijing/Shanghai

Digital Ecosystem (Critical):
- WeChat is the operating system of luxury in China: WeChat Mini-Program handles discovery, appointment booking, wish-list sharing (clients share wish-lists with gifting partners via WeChat), and post-purchase service
- RED (Xiaohongshu): #1 luxury aspiration platform — Aurelle content strategy on RED is managed by China Marketing team; stylists may reshare approved content
- Weibo: Declining relevance for luxury, maintained for KOL relationships
- Douyin (TikTok China): Growing for brand awareness among 18–28 demographic; not yet a sales-conversion platform for Aurelle
- KOL (Key Opinion Leader) Strategy: Aurelle China maintains relationships with 12 Tier-1 KOLs (celebrity-level, >5M followers) and 45 KOCs (Key Opinion Consumers — authentic HNW individuals sharing genuine purchases). KOC strategy is more valuable for conversion than KOL for awareness

Key Dates (China Market):
- January–February: CNY preparation — highest gifting season, 30–35% of H1 revenue
- May 20 (520 Day): Couples gifting — LOVE bracelet peak, equivalent in commercial weight to Valentine's Day
- June 18 (618 Shopping Festival): Digital-led promotions; Aurelle does not discount but activates exclusive digital content and limited online CRM events
- October (Golden Week): Tourism recovery, both domestic travel to boutiques and Mainland visitors to HK/Macau
- November 11 (Singles' Day): Luxury brands have progressively withdrawn from deep discount participation; Aurelle uses this period for digital brand content only
- December: Christmas and New Year gifting, watch and jewellery balanced

Competition in China:
- Bulgari: Aggressive boutique expansion, strong in new wealth tier with Serpenti positioning
- Tiffany (LVMH): Repositioned as higher-tier post-acquisition, direct competition for LOVE-equivalent (T bracelet) client
- Van Cleef: Strongest competitor in High Jewellery and fine jewellery; Alhambra motif has achieved cultural saturation similar to LOVE in some demographics
- Domestic brands (Lao Feng Xiang, Chow Tai Fook): Compete for aspirational tier but are not direct luxury competitors for Aurelle's core positioning
- Key differentiator: Aurelle's dual identity (watches + jewellery under one Maison roof) is a genuine competitive moat — no competitor matches both categories at equivalent depth

HONG KONG

Market Role: Hong Kong serves as Aurelle's global benchmark for retail excellence — highest average transaction value boutique globally (Landmark flagship). It is simultaneously: (1) a domestic luxury market for HK residents, (2) a Mainland tourist destination for cross-border shoppers, (3) a hub for international clients (Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, Western expats).
Revenue Target FY2025: USD 78M (included in Greater China total)
Key Dynamics:
- Landmark Hong Kong Maison: 1,800 sqm, highest per-sqm productivity in APAC. Renovation completed 2024 with expanded HJ salon.
- Cross-border shopping recovery: Mainland visits to HK luxury boutiques recovering to approximately 65% of 2019 levels (FY2025 projection). Simplified CRM data-sharing across CN/HK sub-markets improves client recognition.
- Local HK client: Sophisticated, internationally traveled, watches-informed (proximity to independent watch retail culture). Appreciates technical detail and understated presentation.
- Price differential: With current currency dynamics, HK pricing is approximately 8–12% below equivalent European purchase for Chinese tourists — a commercial argument that must be handled carefully (price shopping diminishes relationship quality; stylists should refocus conversation to relationship and experience value).

MACAU

Market Role: Casino resort ecosystem drives a unique client profile — mainland VHNWIs visiting for entertainment, often in groups, with significant discretionary spend in a compressed timeframe.
Revenue Target FY2025: USD 22M
Key Dynamics:
- Aurelle Macau operates 2 boutiques: Wynn Palace (highest performance) and Galaxy Macau
- Client profile: Very high CLV potential but low retention rate — many clients do not return. CRM capture rate is a critical metric (target: capture full details of 75% of transactions >USD 5,000)
- High Jewellery and High Complication watches perform above their APAC average in Macau — 'event luxury' spend behavior
- Cultural nuance: Many Macau luxury purchases are made under social influence of companion group — social proof from staff about what 'discerning clients choose' is more effective than individual persuasion
- VIP program integration: Aurelle Macau maintains relationships with major casino VIP hosts — with client consent, VIP hosts may make introductions to high-value guests. Protocol: casino introductions must still flow through Aurelle CRM capture within 24 hours.

title: Market Intelligence — Northeast Asia (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan)
content:
Northeast Asia Market Intelligence — Japan, South Korea, Taiwan

JAPAN

Market Overview:
Japan is Aurelle's most mature APAC market and the region's standard-setter for client relationship excellence. Revenue target FY2025: USD 85M. Japan clients demonstrate the highest loyalty metrics in APAC (8% churn rate, average purchase frequency 3.2x per year among Tier 1). The Japanese luxury market is characterized by extreme attention to craft quality, understated aesthetics, discretion in service, and gift-giving culture as a social institution.

Boutique Network:
- Ginza Maison (Tokyo flagship): USD 32M revenue — 2,400 sqm, multiple private salons, dedicated High Jewellery floor. Watch Specialist team of 6.
- Omotesando Boutique (Tokyo): USD 12M — younger client demographic, lifestyle-positioned
- Osaka Shinsaibashi: USD 11M — Kansai wealthy client base, more traditional preferences
- Nagoya Mitsukoshi: USD 8M — manufacturing wealth clientele, strong watch preference
- Fukuoka, Sapporo, Sendai: USD 7M combined — regional boutiques, efficient smaller format

Consumer Profile:
- Primary buyer: Female, 35–55, meticulous purchase decision (average consideration period 6 weeks before first luxury watch purchase)
- Male segment: Watch collector profile — deep knowledge, interested in movement, finishing, historical provenance. Respects technical expertise in sales staff — staff training investment highest requirement.
- Gift culture: Ochugen (July) and Oseibo (December) are institutionalized gift seasons generating significant fragrance and accessories revenue. March graduation and June wedding seasons drive watch and fine jewellery peaks.
- Self-purchase female: 'Jibun-uke' concept (treating oneself) is normalized in Japan — Ballon Bleu 33mm and Trinity necklace are primary self-purchase items.

Key Commercial Behaviors:
- Yen weakness (currently ~150 JPY/USD): Japanese clients have significantly increased outbound luxury purchasing in Europe. Aurelle must compete with the 'Paris trip' purchasing habit. Counter-strategy: emphasize local relationship value, service continuity, Japan-exclusive pieces (small allocation of Panthère special orders and Japan-only gift packaging).
- Returns culture: Japan has the lowest product return rate in APAC — purchases are highly considered and final.
- Punctuality in relationship: If Aurelle commits to a follow-up call, it must happen at the exact agreed time. Any deviation is remembered and damages trust.
- Staff turnover sensitivity: Japanese clients are exceptionally sensitive to stylist changes. CRM transition protocol when a stylist moves must be managed with a formal introduction letter from the outgoing and incoming stylist.

SOUTH KOREA

Market Overview:
South Korea is Aurelle's fastest-growing Northeast Asia market. Revenue target FY2025: USD 62M (+22% YoY). Korean luxury consumption has been dramatically accelerated by the global cultural influence of K-drama, K-pop, and Korean cinema — the 'Hallyu' effect creates a luxury aspiration culture domestically that is powerful and self-reinforcing. Korean HNW clients are younger than other APAC markets (primary luxury buyer: 25–40) and more fashion-influenced in their luxury choices.

Boutique Network:
- Cheongdam Maison (Seoul flagship): USD 24M — The address of Korean luxury. Premium-positioned, attracts ultra-high-net-worth and entertainment industry clients.
- Lotte World Tower: USD 11M — tourist and domestic younger wealth
- Galleria Hannam: USD 9M — growing luxury district, younger HNWI
- Hyundai Seoul: USD 7M — department store format, accessible luxury entry
- Busan (Paradise City): USD 5M — resort luxury, tourist-driven
- Online + Private Client: USD 6M

Consumer Profile:
- K-pop and entertainment industry: Numerous idol and actor brand ambassador adjacencies — when a celebrity is photographed in Aurelle, demand for that specific reference spikes 40–200% within 72 hours. Boutique teams must be briefed on celebrity product association immediately when it occurs.
- Couple culture: Korea's 'couple item' culture (matching or complementary items bought by couples) is a significant commercial behavior. LOVE bracelet his-and-hers, Santos (male) + Ballon Bleu (female) pair — couples' styling narratives are effective.
- Skincare-to-luxury pipeline: Korean consumers are exceptional at quality-tiered investing in personal appearance — the mindset that 'the best version of me uses the best products' translates naturally to luxury watches and jewellery.
- Digital behavior: Instagram-driven discovery (different from China, Koreans use global platforms). KakaoTalk for relationship CRM. Luxury clients expect rapid, personalized response via KakaoTalk message.

TAIWAN

Market Overview:
Revenue target FY2025: USD 28M. Taiwan is a small but sophisticated market with disproportionate per-capita luxury spend among its HNWI tier. The Taiwanese luxury client is internationally traveled, often educated in US/Europe, and values quality and discretion above brand loudness.

Boutique Network:
- Taipei 101 Maison (flagship): USD 18M
- Breeze Center, Xinyi District: USD 7M
- SOGO Zhongxiao: USD 3M

Consumer Profile:
- Preference for understated luxury: Trinity ring significantly outperforms LOVE bracelet in relative terms vs. other APAC markets — the less 'obvious' luxury codes are preferred.
- Watch collectors: Taiwan has a strong community of independent watch collectors — Rotonde complications find their most engaged non-Japanese audience here.
- Cross-strait dynamic: Mainland Chinese visitors to Taiwan (when political conditions allow) are an incremental opportunity; CRM cross-market coordination applies.
- Tech wealth: TSMC and semiconductor industry wealth generation has created a new tier of HNWI with international taste and strong interest in wearable luxury as a signal of refined success.

title: Market Intelligence — Southeast Asia & India
content:
Southeast Asia & India Market Intelligence — APAC Coverage

SINGAPORE

Market Role: Singapore is simultaneously an Aurelle market and the APAC regional headquarters city. Revenue target FY2025: USD 38M. Singapore's luxury market punches far above its population weight due to: (1) Very high per-capita income and wealth concentration, (2) HNWI immigration (Global Investor Programme and family office ecosystem), (3) Tourism-driven spend (Changi hub — luxury tourists pause for boutique visits), (4) Corporate gifting market driven by MNCs headquartered in Singapore.

Boutique Network:
- ION Orchard Maison (flagship): USD 16M — primary VIC engagement venue
- Marina Bay Sands: USD 10M — tourist-dominant, strong watches, Japanese and Chinese tourist high-spend
- Takashimaya Ngee Ann City: USD 7M — domestic client base, mix of resident and expatriate
- Jewel Changi: USD 5M — transit luxury, smaller format

Consumer Profile:
- Multi-cultural complexity: Chinese Singaporean (60%), Malay (15%), Indian (9%), expatriate (significant): each sub-segment has distinct luxury behavior. Chinese Singaporean: LOVE and watches, gifting culture; Indian Singaporean: gold-centric, gravitating toward bridal and Trinity; expatriate (Western): watches and fragrance dominant.
- New wealth immigrants: Ultra-high-net-worth families relocating from China, Indonesia, and India — CRM onboarding of newly arrived HNW residents is a priority initiative (collaboration with private banking partners and family office networks).
- Price sensitivity relative to market: Singapore pricing is relatively competitive versus other APAC markets — clients are informed and may price-compare. Focus on relationship and exclusivity value.

THAILAND

Revenue target FY2025: USD 18M. Bangkok is the key city with two boutiques: ICONSIAM Maison (flagship, USD 11M) and Siam Paragon (USD 7M). Thai luxury market is driven by: local HNWI (industrialist and banking families), Chinese-Thai business elite, and tourism (Phuket, Koh Samui — seasonal boutique consideration for future). Thai clients have a very strong social and cultural relationship with jewelry as personal adornment and status symbol — this aligns naturally with Aurelle's jewellery positioning. Buddhist calendar key dates (Songkran, Makha Bucha) drive gifting. Chinese New Year also significant for Chinese-Thai community. Royal family association with European luxury brands is a cultural reference point — historical brand connections are valued.

PHILIPPINES

Revenue target FY2025: USD 14M. Manila boutiques: Greenbelt 5 Makati (USD 8M), BGC High Street (USD 6M). The Philippines has an extremely concentrated luxury consumer base — an estimated 80% of luxury purchases are made by approximately 200 prominent families whose social networks are tight and interlocked. Word-of-mouth within this community is the single most powerful CRM tool. Event-based selling (private dinners at Ayala mansion properties, social gathering sponsorship) is disproportionately effective vs. any other APAC market. Referral tracking in CRM is mandatory — when a new high-value client cites an existing Aurelle client as their introduction, both must be noted and the existing client recognized with a gratitude gesture.

INDIA

Revenue target FY2025: USD 22M (fastest-growing market, +35% projected YoY). Boutiques: Mumbai DLF Emporio equivalent (flagship, USD 12M), Delhi Select Citywalk equivalent (USD 6M), with Bangalore and Hyderabad boutiques opening Q2 2025 (USD 2M each planned).

India-Specific Context:
- Gold culture: India is the world's largest gold consumer. Aurelle's gold jewellery proposition (LOVE, Trinity, Panthère) aligns with deep cultural resonance — but the positioning must be elevated from 'gold jewelry' to 'heritage gold artistry' to justify price premium vs. local fine jewellers.
- BIS Hallmarking: All gold jewellery sold in India must carry BIS hallmark — this is handled at import/SDC level. Stylists must be able to explain hallmarking to clients as a quality guarantee, not a bureaucratic formality.
- Diamond education: Lab-grown diamond market is growing rapidly in India (35% of all diamond jewelry sold in FY2024 in India was lab-grown). Aurelle exclusively uses natural stones. Stylists must proactively explain the difference: rarity, investment, geological time, emotional legacy. Do not wait for the client to raise it.
- Wedding market: Indian wedding season (Oct–Dec, Feb–May) is the primary commercial driver. Bridal jewellery, men's watches as wedding gifts, and family gifting sets are peak demand. Bridal consultation protocol to be developed for Indian market specifically — typically involves multiple family members, multiple visits, longer decision cycle.
- High-net-worth profile: Tech sector wealth (Bangalore), traditional industrial wealth (Mumbai, Ahmedabad), new-generation entrepreneurs. Younger HNWI is more international, digital-first, interested in watch complications and international luxury status. Older HNWI gravitates toward gold jewellery weight and craftsmanship.

AUSTRALIA

Revenue target FY2025: USD 20M. Boutiques: Sydney Pitt Street Maison (flagship, USD 9M), Melbourne Collins Street (USD 6M), Brisbane (USD 3M), Perth (USD 2M). Australia presents a unique challenge in the Aurelle APAC portfolio: the market has the highest churn rate (24%), lowest emotional luxury attachment, and is most price-sensitive relative to income level among APAC markets. However, Australia is also home to a significant mining, finance, and tech wealth tier that represents a strong opportunity if relationship depth can be established. CRM investment in Australia is being increased for FY2025 — dedicated Australian VIC program targeting 400 Tier 1 clients with event calendar of 8 private experiences (wine region events, art fair partnerships, private sporting access). Watches are the dominant category (54% of AU revenue) — Santos and Ballon Bleu lead. Bridal opportunity is underexploited — Australian engagement ring market is primarily dominated by Tiffany; Aurelle's solitaire collection has room to grow with the right brand investment.

title: Pricing Architecture & Financial Policy APAC
content:
Aurelle APAC Pricing Architecture & Financial Policy

PRICING GOVERNANCE
All Aurelle retail prices are set by European HQ (Paris/Geneva) and expressed in EUR as the base. Local market prices are derived from EUR base using a proprietary pricing conversion methodology that accounts for: import duties, local taxes (VAT/GST/consumption tax), currency hedging positions, competitive benchmarking, and brand positioning consistency. Local prices are reviewed and updated quarterly or triggered by currency moves exceeding 5% from hedge rate baseline.

Market Directors do NOT have authority to change retail prices. They may only apply the 3% service adjustment on High Jewellery (as per DOC-002) and approve complimentary services from the service budget. Any pricing exception requires APAC VP Finance approval with documentation.

PRICE POSITIONING BY MARKET (Indexed to EUR base = 100)
- Switzerland/Europe: 100 (base)
- United Kingdom: 105–108 (post-Brexit duty adjustments)
- Mainland China: 118–125 (import duty 18% on watches, 10% on jewellery, plus 13% VAT, currency management)
- Hong Kong: 103–106 (low duty environment, minimal adjustment)
- Macau: 103–106 (same low duty as HK)
- Japan: 108–115 (consumption tax 10%, yen management, currently consumer-favorable vs. 2022)
- South Korea: 110–116 (14% luxury goods tax + VAT)
- Taiwan: 109–114 (import duty + commodity tax)
- Singapore: 104–107 (GST 9%, low duty regime)
- Thailand: 112–118 (high import duties on luxury goods)
- Philippines: 115–120 (high tariff rates on watches and jewellery)
- Australia: 108–112 (GST 10%, AUD volatility management)
- India: 120–130 (highest duty burden in APAC — 20–25% import duty + 3% GST on gold)

MARGIN STRUCTURE (APAC Average)
- Boutique Gross Margin: 68–72% (range varies by product category)
- High Jewellery gross margin: 74–78%
- Watches (perennial references): 62–66%
- Watches (complications/limited): 70–75%
- Classic Jewellery (LOVE, Trinity perennials): 71–74%
- Fragrances & Accessories: 58–63%
- Private Client Salon (Maison events): 75–79% (higher because: no markdown risk, custom pricing ceiling, ancillary service revenue, lower marketing cost per sale)

These margins are EBITA-level — they include product cost of goods and directly attributable boutique costs (rent, direct staff). Corporate overhead allocation, marketing, and regional infrastructure costs are not reflected.

REVENUE RECOGNITION POLICY
Revenue is recognized at point of purchase for in-boutique transactions (regardless of personalization or engraving delay — the contractual obligation is met at purchase). For bespoke commissions and High Jewellery with modification: revenue recognized at delivery acceptance. For lay-away reservations: the 20% deposit is recognized as deferred revenue; full recognition at purchase completion.

INTER-MARKET PRICING ARBITRAGE
Price shopping by clients across markets is a known behavior — particularly Mainland Chinese clients comparing Mainland prices to HK or European prices. Aurelle's response protocol:
1. Never confirm or deny specific price differences to the client (it invites a negotiation mindset)
2. Redirect to relationship and service value: 'We can ensure your piece is serviced and the relationship maintained wherever you are in the world — that is the value of your Aurelle relationship, not the geography of purchase'
3. Acknowledge that some clients prefer to purchase in their home country for convenience of aftercare
4. If a client explicitly states they are considering purchasing in Europe: flag to Market Director; assess if a special experience (private event, exclusive preview) can retain the purchase domestically without price-matching

CURRENCY RISK MANAGEMENT
All APAC subsidiaries hedge 75% of forecasted foreign currency revenue on a rolling 12-month basis using forward contracts. The remaining 25% is unhedged to allow participation in favorable movements. Hedging policy is managed by APAC Finance — boutique and market teams should not engage in discussions about currency hedging with clients or partners.

TAX REFUNDS (TOURIST PURCHASES)
Markets offering VAT/GST refunds to foreign tourists: Australia (TRS scheme, minimum AUD 300, airport claims), Japan (immediate tax-free shopping for eligible foreign visitors — Aurelle boutiques all participate; eligibility checked at point of sale), Thailand (VAT refund at airport for purchases >THB 5,000). Stylists must be briefed on current eligibility rules per market — incorrect guidance on tax refunds creates significant client relationship issues. Tax refund desks/processes must be explained clearly at point of sale for tourist clients.

title: Supply Chain & Allocation Policy APAC
content:
APAC Supply Chain Management — Inventory & Boutique Allocation Policy

NETWORK OVERVIEW
Aurelle APAC operates a dual-hub supply chain model designed to balance speed-to-market, customs efficiency, and boutique inventory health across 13 markets and 67 boutique/sales points.

Regional Hubs:
- Singapore Distribution Centre (SDC): Primary hub for Southeast Asia (SG, TH, PH, MY, IN, AU). Total warehousing capacity: 12,000 units. Average transit to boutique: 3–5 business days. Customs clearance handled centrally for ASEAN markets.
- Switzerland Direct (ZHD): North Asia (Mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan) supplied directly from Manufacture in Le Brassus/Geneva via Swiss Air Freight. Reason: Lower transit time for high-value items, dedicated customs broker relationships, insurance coverage preferred for pieces >CHF 100,000.
- India Supply Corridor: India is supplied from SDC with a separate customs clearance broker in Mumbai due to BIS hallmarking requirements for gold jewellery. Lead time: 7–10 business days.

PRODUCT CATEGORIES AND SUPPLY APPROACH
- Watches (perennial references — Tank Must, Santos, Ballon Bleu): Forecast-driven replenishment on 8-week rolling cycle. Demand signals from boutique POS integrated with SAP S/4HANA allocation module.
- Watches (limited and complications): Allocated annually by HQ in Geneva based on market quotas. APAC quota negotiated by Regional Supply Chain VP with HQ in November for following year.
- Classic Jewellery (LOVE, Trinity, Juste un Clou perennial): Replenishment-driven, 6-week cycle. Fastest-moving SKUs (LOVE bracelet YG, Trinity ring classic) have dedicated safety stock at SDC of minimum 45 units.
- High Jewellery: Piece-by-piece allocation. Each piece has a unique piece number registered in the Aurelle master inventory system. Cannot be duplicated or substituted. Movement tracked at piece level in real time.
- Fragrances & Accessories: Managed separately through commercial distribution network; not covered under jewellery/watches allocation policy.

ALLOCATION OPTIMIZER PROTOCOL
For limited stock of high-demand references (defined as any reference with waitlist >10 names APAC-wide), the Allocation Optimizer engine applies the following weighted criteria:
1. CRM Waitlist Backlog (35% weight): Stores with confirmed VIC clients awaiting specific pieces receive priority.
2. Sales Velocity (25% weight): 90-day rolling average of units sold per available display day.
3. Boutique Tier (20% weight): Tier 1 Maison flagship > Tier 2 flagships > Standard boutiques.
4. Stock Cover Weeks (20% weight): Stores with <3 weeks cover on a reference trigger urgent allocation flag.
The engine runs weekly (every Monday) and outputs an allocation recommendation reviewed by Regional Supply Chain Manager before execution. Market Directors may appeal allocation decisions within 48 hours with documented VIC justification.

EMERGENCY STOCK PROTOCOL
Boutique Directors may file an Emergency Stock Request (ESR) directly to Switzerland (copy Regional Supply Chain) when:
- A confirmed VIC (Tier 1) has expressed intent to purchase a specific reference not currently in boutique stock
- A High Jewellery event is confirmed with specific piece requirements within 14 days
ESR commitments: Delivery within 5 business days for watches/classic jewellery, 7 business days for High Jewellery (additional security packaging and insurance required). ESR is tracked in CRM as a client commitment — failure to deliver on ESR timeline requires Market Director escalation and client communication within 24 hours of identified delay.

INVENTORY HEALTH TARGETS (FY2025)
- Weeks of Cover target: 10–14 weeks for standard references, 6–8 weeks for limited
- Sell-Through Rate target: >72% per collection within 12 months
- Dead Stock threshold: Any unit unsold for >18 months is reviewed for inter-boutique transfer or repatriation to SDC
- Shrinkage target: <0.05% of total inventory value (luxury-grade security protocol mandatory)

INTER-BOUTIQUE TRANSFERS
Boutique Directors may request transfers from neighboring boutiques within the same market (e.g., Shanghai Plaza 66 → Shanghai Jing An) without Regional Supply Chain approval for pieces valued below USD 30,000. Cross-market transfers (e.g., HK → Singapore) require Regional Supply Chain approval and customs documentation. High Jewellery pieces: any transfer requires security escort and HQ notification regardless of value.
